Why Alpha Factor Signal Tranduction?

Haploid S. cerevisiae of opposite mating type can mate and form diploids. Mating is stimulated by the release of small peptide pheromones, a factor from MATa cells and a factor from MATa cells, which prepare cells of the opposite type for mating. When haploid cells are exposed to pheromone they undergo several responses, including cell cycle arrest, induction of mating genes, changes in nuclear architecture, and polarized growth toward the mating partner. The signal transduction pathway that regulates these responses provides one of the best-characterized systems for studying extra- to intra-cellular information flow and processing. Analysis of the pathway is facilitated by its genetic accessibility and recently, by the availability of genome scale expression, protein interaction, and DNA-protein interaction data (see for example, Gasch et al., 2000; Hughes et al., 2000; Hazbun & Fields, 2001; Ren et al., 2000).

Because it continues to be studied actively, the pathway is frequently reviewed (Dohlman & Thorner, 2001; Gustin et al., 1998). Here are some basics. The yeast pheromone signal transduction pathway is made up of a hetptahelical transmembrane receptor, a G-protein complex, a mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) complex, and a number of effector protein kinases and gene regulatory proteins. Activation of the pathway leads to induction of the pheromone response genes and cell cycle arrest. Figure 1 provides an overview diagram of the pathway in MATa cells.

In MATa cells, mating pheromone binds to and activates Ste2 and induces dissociation of Gpa1 from the Ste4/Ste18 complex. The dissociated Ste4/Ste18 dimer recruits Ste5 and the kinase Ste20. Ste5 is a scaffold protein that complexes with the components of the MAPK cascade: Ste11, the MAPK kinase kinase, Ste7, the MAPK kinase and Fus3, the MAPK. Ste20, now in close proximity to its substrate, Ste11, phosphorylates Ste11 and initiates the kinase cascade. Ste11 in turn phosphorylates Ste7, which phosphorylates Fus3. Once Fus3 is phosphorylated, it translocates to the nucleus and activates the transcription factor Ste12. Unphosphorylated Ste12 is bound by Dig1 and Dig2. Fus3 phosphorylates Dig1 and Dig2, which then dissociate from Ste12. Ste12 then becomes phosphorylated, activating it, and induces transcription of pheromone responsive genes (encoding proteins needed for mating). Fus3 also phosphorylates and activates Far1, which inhibits Cdc28, the sole cerevisiae cyclin dependent kinase, arresting the cells in G1. Other details of the core pathway, including its poorly understood dynamic aspects, are given in Appendix 3.

We believe that this genetic regulatory network is ideal for applying existing experimental methods, for developing new genomic technologies, and for coupling their observational powers within an integrative computational framework. The following reasons support this network as a test subject. 1) The level of system description is greater than that for almost any other eukaryotic process, allowing us to develop models and simulations that fairly represent contemporary biological knowledge. 2) The process and the organism are highly tractable experimentally, and we are good at manipulating them; this facilitates the development of new functional genomic experimental methods that can then be transferred to other organisms. 3) The speed of experimentation in this organism makes this system well-suited to rapid iterative cycles linking new experimentation to new computation. 4) Numerous aspects of the biology of the pathway make it possible for us to use it to address significant scientific questions about its system behavior while en route to our modeling goals.

Thus, in the Alpha Project, we will study the response to mating pheromone at the level of the numbers of molecules and their specific reactions. We will embody our understanding in simulations that compute the behavior of single cells responding to the stimulus. We will undertake cycles of experimentation and simulation to resolve discrepancies between observations and predictions. If eventually, the results of our simulations match those of experiments, including all perturbation experiments, we will cautiously consider the representation, and our understanding of the system at this level of resolution, ?complete?.

Experimental and simulation methods developed will be applicable to other prokaryotic and eukaryotic systems, including those in human cells that are involved in diseases. We expect that the lessons learned from this combined work, both scientific and sociological, will be widely applicable. The overall scientific outcomes should include an increased ability to identify points within pathways that can be targeted to achieve large changes in system output, and points at which multiple small perturbations can achieve the same effect. The findings should aid the ability to devise effective interventions in regulatory pathways for purposes of medicine and engineering.
